title: Frontend serves pages to unauthenticated users — no authentication gate exists
body:
## Issue description

The web client (`client/`) is fully accessible to any visitor without any authentication. There is no login flow, no Microsoft Entra ID / MSAL integration, and no guard that redirects unauthenticated users to an identity provider. The deployed application renders `Home.tsx` immediately for any browser that loads the URL, regardless of whether the user is signed in.

This directly violates the non-negotiable security-first design principle documented in `.github/copilot-instructions.md` and `docs/architecture/overview.md`:

> All authenticated requests are validated against a token issued by Microsoft Entra ID / External ID / B2C. No production authentication shortcuts are permitted.

The backend API already enforces Bearer-token authentication on every `/api/v1/*` route via `EntraTokenValidator` (`src/shared/auth/entra-token-validator.ts`). The frontend has no equivalent guard.

## High-level requirements / Expected behavior

- Install `@azure/msal-browser` and `@azure/msal-react` in the client.
- Configure `PublicClientApplication` from `VITE_AZURE_CLIENT_ID` and `VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID` environment variables (never hard-coded).
- Wrap the React root in `MsalProvider`.
- Any route or page that renders application data must be wrapped in an `AuthenticatedTemplate` / `UnauthenticatedTemplate` guard (or an equivalent `useMsal`-based hook).
- Unauthenticated users must be redirected to the Entra ID login page before any application content is rendered.
- After a successful login, the client acquires an access token scoped to the backend API and attaches it as `Authorization: Bearer <token>` on every `/api/v1/` request.
- A logout action must clear the MSAL session and redirect to the login page.
- No client or tenant ID is hard-coded in source; both are read from build-time environment variables.

## Additional notes

- The Vite dev server already proxies `/api` to `http://localhost:8080` (`client/vite.config.ts`), so token injection need only happen in the client's API layer.
- The backend `EntraTokenValidator` validates `oid`/`sub` and `preferred_username`/`name` claims. The frontend token scope must match the backend's expected audience (`AZURE_CLIENT_ID`).
- `createAuthMiddleware` in `src/shared/auth/auth-middleware.ts` throws at startup when `NODE_ENV=production` and no validator is supplied. The same fail-fast principle must apply to the frontend: if the required MSAL environment variables are absent the app must refuse to start rather than silently serve unauthenticated pages.
- Test coverage: use `@azure/msal-react`'s `useMsalAuthentication` test utilities or mock `PublicClientApplication` in Vitest to cover authenticated and unauthenticated rendering paths.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `@azure/msal-browser` and `@azure/msal-react` are installed as client production dependencies.
- [ ] `MsalProvider` wraps the React app root with a `PublicClientApplication` configured from environment variables.
- [ ] Any page that renders user data is wrapped in an authentication guard; unauthenticated users see only a redirect or a minimal login prompt — no application data.
- [ ] After login, the client attaches `Authorization: Bearer <token>` on all `/api/v1/` requests.
- [ ] Logout clears the MSAL session and returns the user to the login page.
- [ ] `VITE_AZURE_CLIENT_ID` and `VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID` are documented in `.env.example`.
- [ ] The app fails fast (throws at initialisation) when required MSAL env vars are absent.
- [ ] Component tests cover authenticated vs. unauthenticated rendering.
- [ ] CI passes with the new dependencies and tests.
